title: Chapter 2: The Château
content: The TGV split France in two.

On the left: Paris, receding — Haussmann rooftops, crane arms, the concrete collar of the banlieues. On the right: nothing yet, just the gray smear of suburbs decomposing into farmland. Jane pressed her forehead to the glass and watched the palette change. Urban ash gave way to limestone gold, flint-white barns, fields the color of unspun flax. The sky cracked open into a blue so pastoral it felt performative, like a backdrop rolled down for tourists.

She should have been looking at it — the Loire unfurling its silver thread, the châteaux dreaming on their lawns. Instead, she was reading a lookbook she'd stolen from a press kit at a fashion week afterparty eighteen months ago.

Thornfield. Autumn/Winter. The cover was matte black, embossed with the house's logo — a stylized thorn wreath, simple as a watermark. Inside, the construction was impeccable: a charcoal wool coat with invisible seaming that followed the body's tension lines like architecture; a silk column dress in bruise-purple, cut on the bias so it moved like water finding its level; a tailcoat in black cashmere whose lapels were hand-rolled, not pressed, giving the illusion of something almost alive at the collar.
Every page. Every piece. The copy beneath read like hagiography: *Rochester's vision. Rochester's uncompromising eye. Rochester's refusal to compromise craft for commerce.*

Jane turned pages slowly, her thumb finding the credit lines at the bottom of each spread. Edward Rochester — Creative Director. Edward Rochester — Founder. Edward Rochester — CEO. She scanned the production credits, the studio acknowledgments, the tiny type where pattern cutters and drapers and sample machinists were supposed to live.
Nothing.

Not a single other designer credited. Not a head of atelier, not a premier d'atelier, not even a studio name. Every stitch, every innovation, every structural gamble attributed to one man, as though he'd threaded the needle himself and sewn each garment with his own hands.
Jane folded down the corner of the page. She had a system for these — her own private taxonomy. This one she marked *solipsism*, though she was open to upgrading it to *control*.
She felt the château before she saw it.

The taxi turned off the D952 and onto a private road flanked by plane trees so old their canopies had braided into a single green cathedral. The avenue ended at gates — wrought iron, original, tall enough to admit a carriage. Beyond them, Château Thornfield rose in honey-colored limestone, its symmetry so perfect it looked drawn rather than built. Two stories of tall windows, a mansard roof in slate, ivy climbing the west wing with the discipline of a soldier. The central pediment bore a carved wreath — thorns, of course — and nothing else. No name, no date. Just the symbol, assertive as a signature.

Jane paid the driver and stepped out into cold air that tasted of chalk and cut grass. The silence was immediate and complete, the kind of quiet that had been engineered, not happened. She pulled her suitcase across the gravel — the sound was obscene, loud as a wrong note — and reached the front door just as it opened.
"Oh my God. Oh my *God*."

The girl was small, blonde, and vibrating at a frequency that suggested she'd either had four espressos or was naturally configured for enthusiasm. She was wearing a Thornfield sample — the telltale hand-rolled lapels — that was slightly too big for her, as if she'd borrowed it from someone who hadn't wanted to part with it.

"You're Jane Eyre. You're the TikTok lady. Your Martin Margiela deconstruction video — the one where you explained the exposed seams as a trauma narrative — it made me *cry*." She grabbed Jane's suitcase with both hands. "I'm Adèle. Adèle Varens. I'm the intern. Well, I'm Mr. Rochester's intern, but he said I could show you around because he's in Milan until tomorrow and I said *absolutely*, because I've watched all your videos, even the old ones from when your lighting was bad—"
"Thank you," Jane said, and meant it. The girl's earnestness was disarming, a counterpoint to the building's severity.

Inside, the château's bones were eighteenth century — herringbone parquet, ceiling roses, the proportions of a place built to demonstrate wealth through grace. But everything else had been stripped and replaced. The walls were white plaster, unbroken by moldings. The furniture was glass and brushed steel. The lighting was recessed, color-corrected to the same 5000 Kelvin used in design studios, and it bleached the limestone of warmth, turning history into a showroom.
It was beautiful. It was also sterile — a wound dressed in cashmere.

"This is the design floor," Adèle said, leading her through a vast open space where drafting tables stood in rows like altars. Each one was pristine, bare except for a single lamp and a steel ruler. Three people worked, heads down, silent. "Mr. Rochester likes it quiet. He says design is a discipline, not a conversation."
Jane counted the cameras. Two in the corridor they'd just left, one above the elevator, three across the design floor's ceiling — black domes, not trying to hide. She said nothing.

"The fabric library!" Adèle pushed through a door and the air changed — suddenly warm, alive, thick with the smell of wool and silk and the faint chemical sweetness of treated leather. Floor-to-ceiling shelves held bolts arranged by color family, each labeled in a hand so precise it could have been set in type. Jane ran her fingers along a bolt of forest-green wool crepe and felt the immediate, involuntary response of her palm — the muscle memory of a woman who'd spent ten years with fabric in her hands.

"That's from the archive," Adèle said, watching her. "Mr. Rochester keeps the active collections in the studio, but the heritage fabrics are here. You'll be working with these, I think. He didn't tell me much." A beat. "He doesn't tell me much about anything."

The long gallery was next — a corridor of glass and limestone where Edward Rochester apparently worked. A single desk faced the window. A chair, pushed back precisely. No personal effects. The view was of a formal garden, geometric as a theorem, and beyond it, a wall of yew trees so dense they looked black.
Jane counted two more cameras.
Her suite was on the second floor, east wing. Adèle opened the door with a key card — not a key, Jane noted; the lock was electronic, which meant it logged — and stepped aside.
"I'll let you settle. Dinner's at seven, if you want, or I can have something sent up? Mrs. Poole makes a— well, she doesn't *make* anything, she heats things. But it's fine. It's all fine here."
Jane thanked her again, and the door closed with a soft, expensive click, and she was alone.

The room was gorgeous. Oatmeal linens on a wide bed, cashmere throws folded at the foot, a marble bathroom visible through a half-open door. The walls were the same white plaster as the rest of the château, hung with nothing — no art, no mirror, no personality. The furniture was minimal and exquisite: a single chair, a nightstand, a wardrobe of pale ash. The carpet was wool, neutral, plush enough to swallow sound.

It smelled like a hotel room. Not cleaned — *prepared*. The chemical newness of fresh detergent overlaying traces of lavender air freshener, both fighting to mask whatever had been there before. The air was climate-controlled to the degree, and it sat on Jane's skin like a second layer, synthetic and close.
She opened her suitcase on the bed.

The clothes inside were hers, curated over a decade — a Vivienne Westwood blazer in Prince of Wales check, worn at the cuffs; a Marimekko dress in violent poppy-red; a vintage Courrèges shift in white vinyl; silk scarves from Portobello Road in colors that didn't match and didn't care. Against the suite's oatmeal-and-ash neutrality, they looked like evidence. Like a crime scene photo of an actual human being who existed in color and pattern and memory, laid out against a room designed for someone who didn't.
Jane hung the Westwood blazer in the wardrobe and stopped.
The left side of the rail was already occupied.

Six garments — three tops, two trousers, a dress — hung on Thornfield hangers, the kind with the logo embossed on the wood. They were in her size. They were all neutral: charcoal, slate, ivory, stone. They were beautiful. She pulled one down — a cashmere sweater in heather gray — and checked the label. Sample. Not retail. From the current season.

Someone had gone through her measurements — she'd submitted them for the contract, for "work attire considerations" — and selected a wardrobe. Someone had removed the empty hangers that would have revealed the gap. Someone had stood in this room before her and decided what she should wear.
Jane put the sweater back and hung her red dress beside it. The contrast was violent.

The tour continued after she'd unpacked — or rather, after she'd positioned her belongings like small acts of resistance throughout the suite. Adèle met her in the corridor, slightly calmer now, and walked her through the east wing: the kitchens (industrial, stainless, spotless), a sitting room she catalogued as *surveillance hub* based on the monitor feed visible through a half-shut door, and a secondary staircase that led down and away from the main house.
They were passing the stairhead when Jane saw her.

A woman stood in the doorway at the bottom of the first flight. Solid. Gray. Gray hair cut bluntly at the jaw, gray cardigan over a gray dress, gray eyes that didn't so much look at Jane as register her — the way a scanner registers a document, without interest or opinion. She was perhaps forty-five, perhaps older; her face had the deliberate blankness of someone who'd practiced not being remembered.

The doorway behind her led downward. Into the basement, Jane assumed — the house's foundations, its understructure. The woman's hand was on the doorframe, and when she noticed Jane looking, she didn't flinch, didn't startle, didn't react at all. She simply stepped back and pulled the door shut.
The click was soft, final, and professional.
"Who's that?" Jane asked, keeping her voice light.
Adèle, three steps ahead, turned. "Oh — that's Mrs. Poole. She looks after the... storage."

The hesitation was microscopic. A fraction of a syllable, a breath-weight pause between *the* and *storage* that repositioned the entire sentence. Not "the storage" as in a place where things were kept. *The* storage — as in something particular, something Adèle had almost named differently and then thought better of.
"She makes dinner," Adèle added, brightly, as though this was the important thing. "Well. She heats dinner."

Jane filed the pause. Filed the door. Filed the gray woman's deliberate, practiced blankness. She'd known women like that in archives — the kind who stood between you and something, and who made standing feel like nothing personal, just function.

Night came suddenly. The Loire sky darkened from blue to violet to black in what felt like fifteen minutes, and the château's automated lighting clicked on in precise intervals — corridor, then stairwell, then the yew-hedge gardens below — as if the house was performing its own security sweep.
Jane lay in the oatmeal bed and could not sleep.

The suite was too quiet. Not peaceful-quiet — *attenuated* quiet, as if the climate control and the acoustic insulation and the wool carpet were collaborating to remove every trace of human occupancy. She could hear her own heartbeat. She could hear the soft tick of her watch on the nightstand. She could hear nothing else, and the nothing was vigilant, the silence of a place that listened.

She got up. The floor was cold under her bare feet, and she resented the chill — another engineered discomfort, a house that wanted you in bed, contained, horizontal. She pulled on the gray cashmere sweater from the wardrobe (her own Westwood blazer was closer, but she chose the Thornfield piece, and she didn't know why, and that bothered her) and padded into the corridor.

The château at night was different. The design-floor lighting was lowered to a faint blue-white glow, and the glass partitions reflected her own shape back at her — a small woman in gray moving through a gray house. The cameras watched, their indicator lights steady red pinpricks. She didn't wave at them.
She was halfway down the corridor toward the secondary staircase when she heard it.
*Snk. Snk. Snk.*

Scissors. The sound was unmistakable to anyone who'd spent time in an atelier — the clean, rhythmic bite of blades through fabric. Not snipping, not hacking. Cutting. Deliberate, measured, the pace of someone who knew exactly what they were doing.
The sound came from below. From the basement. From *storage*.

Jane stopped. The corridor was silent except for the cutting — *snk, snk, snk* — and she realized she could feel it in the floor, the faint vibration transmitted through the limestone, through the steel substructure, through her bare feet. Someone was down there. Working. At — she checked her phone — one-forty-seven in the morning.
She crossed to the secondary staircase. The door was closed, as it had been when Grace Poole shut it. She pressed her ear to the wood.
*Snk. Snk. Sn—*
The sound stopped.
Not trailed off. Not finished a cut. Stopped. Mid-rhythm. Mid-breath. The silence that replaced it was so total it had texture — dense, watchful, present.

Jane held still. Her pulse was loud in her ears and she willed it quiet, willed herself to be the kind of person who could stand at a door and hear nothing and not fill the nothing with panic. Ten seconds. Twenty. The basement was perfectly, absolutely silent.
Someone had heard her listening.
She stepped back from the door. Walked to her suite. Closed it behind her and locked it — an electronic lock, she remembered, that logged every action. Let it log.
She sat on the edge of the bed and opened her phone. The Wi-Fi icon spun, searching, and displayed: *Thornfield_Guest — Down for maintenance.* She switched to cellular. Two bars — enough.
She typed notes into her phone, thumbs quick and certain:
*House is surveilled. Basement is occupied. Housekeeper is a guard, not a cleaner. And someone is sewing at 2 AM who isn't supposed to exist.*
She stared at the screen. Then she added one more line:
*Wi-Fi down. Convenient.*

She put the phone face-down on the nightstand and lay back. The ceiling was white and featureless, a blank page. She listened for the scissors — strained toward the sound the way you lean toward a conversation at the next table, desperate to catch the end of a sentence that explains everything.
They didn't start again.

The silence stretched, elastic and patient, and somewhere in the basement of Château Thornfield, someone was very deliberately not cutting fabric anymore, and Jane understood — with the same certainty she brought to identifying a designer by the weight of a seam — that they had stopped because she had listened.
The dark was listening back.
